name: Web Crawler
overview:
A web crawler systematically browses the web, downloading and indexing web pages for a search engine. The
crawler must discover new pages by following links, avoid crawling the same page twice, respect robots.txt and
crawl delays, handle diverse content types, and scale to crawl billions of pages. The crawler must be polite (not
overload web servers), fault-tolerant, and able to prioritize important pages.

inScope: Extract and follow links from crawled pages

items: Crawl 1 billion pages per month (~385 pages/sec sustained)
Politeness: max 1 request/sec per domain; respect robots.txt crawl delays
URL deduplication must handle 1B+ URLs with <0.1% false positive rate
Fault tolerant: worker failures must not lose queued URLs or crawled content
Horizontally scalable: add crawler workers without coordination overhead
Crawl freshness: high-PageRank pages recrawled more frequently than low-priority pages

entities.name: CrawlJob
entities.schema:
{
  url: string,
  priority: float,  // PageRank estimate
  depth: int,
  discovered_from: string,  // parent URL
  scheduled_at: timestamp,
  last_crawled: timestamp,
  crawl_delay: int  // from robots.txt
}

entities.name: CrawledPage
entities.schema:
{
  url: string,

  content_hash: SHA256,
  html: string,
  links: [string],
  crawled_at: timestamp,
  http_status: int,
  content_type: string
}

approaches.title: Simple FIFO Queue
approaches.description: Store URLs in a FIFO queue. Crawl in order of discovery. No prioritization. Low-quality pages are crawled before high-quality ones. No politeness (multiple requests to same domain in rapid succession).
approaches.tradeoff: No prioritization; no politeness; poor crawl quality.

approaches.title: Priority Queue per Domain
approaches.description: Maintain a priority queue per domain. Crawl high-priority URLs first. Enforce crawl delay between requests to the same domain. Better quality and politeness. But managing millions of per-domain queues is complex.
approaches.tradeoff: Complex queue management; memory-intensive for millions of domains.

approaches.title: Two-Level URL Frontier
approaches.description: Front queues: multiple priority queues (HIGH/MEDIUM/LOW) based on PageRank estimate. Back queues: one queue per domain, enforcing crawl delay. A selector picks from front queues and routes to the appropriate back queue. Workers pull from back queues. If a domain's back queue is empty, the worker picks from another domain's queue. URL deduplication uses a distributed Bloom filter (Redis BF module).
approaches.tradeoff: Complex two-level architecture; Bloom filter has false positives (slight re-crawling).

approaches.title: Exact URL Match in DB
approaches.description: Store all crawled URLs in a DB. Check before crawling. At 1B URLs, the DB becomes a bottleneck. Each URL check requires a DB query.
approaches.tradeoff: DB bottleneck at 1B URLs; slow lookup.

approaches.title: Bloom Filter
approaches.description: Use a Bloom filter to check if a URL has been crawled. False positive rate ~0.1%. O(1) lookup. Memory: ~1.2 GB for 1B URLs at 0.1% FPR. Distributed across Redis cluster. False positives cause ~0.1% of URLs to be skipped (acceptable).
approaches.tradeoff: False positives cause slight under-crawling; Bloom filter cannot be shrunk.

approaches.title: Bloom Filter + Content Hash Dedup
approaches.description: URL-level dedup: Bloom filter for fast URL lookup. Content-level dedup: SHA256 hash of page content. If content hash already seen, skip indexing (but still follow links). Content dedup catches mirror sites and syndicated content that have different URLs but identical content.
approaches.tradeoff: Two-level dedup adds complexity; content hashing requires downloading the page first.

components.name: URL Frontier
components.responsibility: Two-level priority queue; front queues by priority; back queues by domain

components.name: Crawler Workers
components.responsibility: Fetch pages; extract links; publish to Kafka; respect robots.txt

components.name: DNS Resolver
components.responsibility: Caches DNS lookups per domain; reduces DNS query overhead

components.name: Bloom Filter (Redis)
components.responsibility: URL deduplication; distributed across Redis cluster

components.name: Link Extractor
components.responsibility: Parses HTML; extracts links; normalizes URLs; publishes to URL Frontier

components.name: Indexer
components.responsibility: Processes crawled content; builds inverted index for search

components.name: PageRank Calculator
components.responsibility: Batch job computing PageRank from link graph; updates URL priorities

questions.title: 2. URL Normalization
questions.content:
URLs must be normalized before deduplication to avoid crawling the same page multiple times with different
URL representations. Normalization steps: (1) Lowercase scheme and host, (2) Remove default ports (80 for
HTTP, 443 for HTTPS), (3) Remove URL fragments (#section), (4) Sort query parameters alphabetically, (5)
Remove tracking parameters (utm_source, fbclid, etc.), (6) Resolve relative URLs to absolute.

questions.title: 3. Handling Redirects
questions.content:
HTTP 301/302 redirects are followed up to 5 hops. The final URL after redirects is stored in the Bloom filter. If
the redirect chain leads to an already-crawled URL, the crawl is skipped. Redirect chains are stored to detect
redirect loops (A → B → A). Circular redirects are detected and the URL is marked as an error.

questions.title: 4. Distributed Crawling
questions.content:
The crawler is distributed across 1000 worker nodes. URL assignment uses consistent hashing by domain: all
URLs from the same domain go to the same worker node. This ensures crawl delay enforcement is local (no
coordination needed between workers for the same domain). Workers are stateless — they pull from the URL
Frontier and push results to Kafka.

questions.title: 5. Recrawling Strategy
questions.content:
Pages must be recrawled periodically to detect updates. Recrawl frequency is based on: (1) Page change
frequency (estimated from previous crawls), (2) Page importance (PageRank), (3) Content type (news pages
recrawled hourly, static pages monthly). The URL Frontier stores the next_crawl_time for each URL and
re-queues it when the time arrives.

Discover · Fetch · Extract · Index

new URLs

link graph

keyFlows: ① URL Frontier dispatches URL to Crawler (consistent hash by domain)
② Crawler: check Bloom filter → fetch page → publish to Kafka
③ Link Extractor: parse HTML → normalize URLs → add to URL Frontier
④ Raw HTML stored in S3 → Indexer builds inverted index
⑤ PageRank batch job updates URL priorities in URL Frontier

whatToStudyFurther.content:
Expert Note: Study the Mercator web crawler architecture: the two-level URL frontier design, how it
balances politeness with crawl throughput, and how it handles the scale of the web.
Expert Note: Learn about PageRank: the original Google algorithm, how it models the web as a Markov
chain, and how it's computed iteratively using the power method.
Expert Note: Understand robots.txt: the Robots Exclusion Protocol, how to parse and cache robots.txt files,
and the legal implications of ignoring robots.txt.
Expert Note: Study consistent hashing for URL assignment: how to distribute URLs across crawler nodes
by domain hash, and how to handle node failures without re-crawling.
Expert Note: Learn about web content deduplication: SimHash for near-duplicate detection, how to handle
mirror sites and content syndication, and the trade-off between dedup accuracy and crawl coverage.
